1_Session_and_Database_Information.description: Selects the default database for subsequent queries in the current session.
Lists the databases available on the MySQL server.

2_Data_Types_Numeric.description: Very small integer. Signed range: -128 to 127. Unsigned range: 0 to 255. Storage: 1 byte.
Small integer. Signed range: -32,768 to 32,767. Unsigned range: 0 to 65,535. Storage: 2 bytes.
Medium-sized integer. Range approx +/- 8 million. Storage: 3 bytes.
Standard integer. Signed range: approx -2 billion to 2 billion. Storage: 4 bytes.
Large integer. Signed range: approx -9 quintillion to 9 quintillion. Storage: 8 bytes.
Single-precision floating point number. Approximate values.
Double-precision floating point number. More precise than FLOAT.
Exact fixed-point number. Essential for financial data. Precision is total digits, scale is digits after decimal.
A bit-field type. M is the number of bits per value (1 to 64).

3_Data_Types_String_Binary.description: Fixed-length string (0-255). Right-padded with spaces to specific length.
Variable-length string (0-65,535). Uses only as much space as needed plus length bytes.
Max size 4GB. Suitable for massive logs or serialized objects.
String object with a value chosen from a list of permitted values.
String object that can have zero or more values from a list.

4_Data_Types_Date_Time_JSON.description: Format: YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS. Converted to UTC for storage, back to current timezone for retrieval. Range: 1970 to 2038.
Stores JSON documents efficiently. Allows validation and fast lookups.

5_Core_Querying_Select_Variations.description: Retrieves all columns and all rows.
Removes duplicate values from the result set.

5_Core_Querying_Select_Variations.syntax: SELECT col_name AS alias_name FROM table_name;
5_Core_Querying_Select_Variations.description: Renames a column in the result set.
5_Core_Querying_Select_Variations.example: SELECT price * 0.9 AS discounted_price FROM products;

5_Core_Querying_Select_Variations.syntax: SELECT t.col FROM table_name AS t;
5_Core_Querying_Select_Variations.description: Renames a table for the duration of the query (useful in joins).
5_Core_Querying_Select_Variations.example: SELECT p.name FROM products AS p;

5_Core_Querying_Select_Variations.syntax: SELECT * FROM table ORDER BY col ASC;
5_Core_Querying_Select_Variations.description: Sorts results from smallest to largest.
5_Core_Querying_Select_Variations.example: SELECT * FROM users ORDER BY name ASC;

5_Core_Querying_Select_Variations.syntax: SELECT * FROM table ORDER BY col DESC;
5_Core_Querying_Select_Variations.description: Sorts results from largest to smallest.
5_Core_Querying_Select_Variations.example: SELECT * FROM users ORDER BY score DESC;

5_Core_Querying_Select_Variations.syntax: SELECT * FROM table ORDER BY col1 DESC, col2 ASC;
5_Core_Querying_Select_Variations.description: Sorts by first column, then by second column if first column values are equal.
5_Core_Querying_Select_Variations.example: SELECT * FROM employees ORDER BY department_id ASC, salary DESC;

5_Core_Querying_Select_Variations.description: Restricts the number of rows returned.

5_Core_Querying_Select_Variations.syntax: SELECT * FROM table LIMIT count OFFSET skip;
5_Core_Querying_Select_Variations.example: SELECT * FROM posts LIMIT 10 OFFSET 20; -- Page 3 of 10-item pages

6_Filtering_Where_Operators.syntax: WHERE col != value OR WHERE col <> value
WHERE col > value / WHERE col < value
WHERE col >= value / WHERE col <= value

6_Filtering_Where_Operators.example: SELECT * FROM cars WHERE make = 'Ford' AND color = 'Red';

6_Filtering_Where_Operators.description: At least one condition must be true.
6_Filtering_Where_Operators.example: SELECT * FROM cars WHERE color = 'Red' OR color = 'Blue';
SELECT * FROM users WHERE NOT status = 'active';

6_Filtering_Where_Operators.syntax: WHERE col BETWEEN val1 AND val2
6_Filtering_Where_Operators.example: SELECT * FROM orders WHERE order_date BETWEEN '2023-01-01' AND '2023-12-31';

6_Filtering_Where_Operators.description: Matches any value in the list.
6_Filtering_Where_Operators.example: SELECT * FROM users WHERE id IN (1, 5, 10);
SELECT * FROM tasks WHERE completed_at IS NULL;
SELECT * FROM tasks WHERE completed_at IS NOT NULL;
SELECT * FROM users WHERE email LIKE '%@gmail.com';
SELECT * FROM products WHERE sku REGEXP '^A-[0-9]{3}$';

7_Aggregate_Functions.description: Returns the number of rows or non-NULL values.
Returns the number of unique non-NULL values.
Returns the total sum of a numeric column.
Concatenates values from a group into a single string.

8_Grouping_and_Having.syntax: SELECT col, AGG_FUNC(col) FROM table GROUP BY col;
8_Grouping_and_Having.description: Groups rows that have the same values into summary rows.
8_Grouping_and_Having.example: SELECT status, COUNT(*) FROM orders GROUP BY status;

8_Grouping_and_Having.syntax: SELECT col FROM table GROUP BY col HAVING condition;
8_Grouping_and_Having.description: Filters groups based on aggregate conditions (like WHERE for groups).
8_Grouping_and_Having.example: SELECT user_id, SUM(total) FROM orders GROUP BY user_id HAVING SUM(total) > 1000;

8_Grouping_and_Having.description: Adds a summary row (super-aggregate) to the result set.
8_Grouping_and_Having.example: SELECT year, SUM(sales) FROM report GROUP BY year WITH ROLLUP;

9_Control_Flow_Functions.description: Returns the first non-NULL value in the list.
9_Control_Flow_Functions.example: SELECT COALESCE(phone, email, 'No Contact') FROM users;

9_Control_Flow_Functions.description: Returns the expression if not NULL, otherwise returns alt_value.
Returns NULL if expr1 = expr2, otherwise returns expr1.
9_Control_Flow_Functions.example: SELECT NULLIF(value, 0); -- Avoid division by zero

9_Control_Flow_Functions.description: Returns a value based on a condition.
9_Control_Flow_Functions.example: SELECT IF(qty > 0, 'In Stock', 'Out of Stock') FROM products;

9_Control_Flow_Functions.syntax: CASE WHEN cond THEN val ELSE val END
9_Control_Flow_Functions.example: SELECT CASE WHEN age < 18 THEN 'Minor' WHEN age < 65 THEN 'Adult' ELSE 'Senior' END FROM people;

11_Joins_Advanced.syntax: SELECT * FROM t1 INNER JOIN t2 ON t1.col = t2.col;
11_Joins_Advanced.description: Returns records that have matching values in both tables.
11_Joins_Advanced.example: SELECT * FROM users JOIN orders ON users.id = orders.user_id;

11_Joins_Advanced.syntax: SELECT * FROM t1 LEFT JOIN t2 ON t1.col = t2.col;
11_Joins_Advanced.description: Returns all records from left table, and matched records from right.
11_Joins_Advanced.example: SELECT * FROM users LEFT JOIN orders ON users.id = orders.user_id;

11_Joins_Advanced.syntax: SELECT * FROM t1 RIGHT JOIN t2 ON t1.col = t2.col;
11_Joins_Advanced.description: Returns all records from right table, and matched records from left.
11_Joins_Advanced.example: SELECT * FROM users RIGHT JOIN orders ON users.id = orders.user_id;

11_Joins_Advanced.syntax: SELECT * FROM t1 CROSS JOIN t2;
11_Joins_Advanced.description: Cartesian product. Returns every combination of rows from both tables.
11_Joins_Advanced.example: SELECT sizes.name, colors.name FROM sizes CROSS JOIN colors;

11_Joins_Advanced.syntax: SELECT * FROM t1 NATURAL JOIN t2;
11_Joins_Advanced.description: Joins tables based on columns with the same name and type automatically.
11_Joins_Advanced.example: SELECT * FROM employees NATURAL JOIN departments;

11_Joins_Advanced.syntax: SELECT * FROM t1 JOIN t2 USING (col_name);
11_Joins_Advanced.description: Simplified ON clause when column names are identical.
11_Joins_Advanced.example: SELECT * FROM orders JOIN users USING (user_id);

11_Joins_Advanced.syntax: SELECT ... FROM t1 JOIN (SELECT ...) AS alias ON condition;
11_Joins_Advanced.description: Joining a table with a temporary result set from a subquery.
11_Joins_Advanced.example: SELECT u.name, s.total FROM users u JOIN (SELECT user_id, SUM(amt) as total FROM orders GROUP BY user_id) s ON u.id = s.user_id;

12_Subqueries_and_CTEs.example: SELECT * FROM products WHERE price > (SELECT AVG(price) FROM products);
SELECT * FROM users WHERE id IN (SELECT user_id FROM orders);

12_Subqueries_and_CTEs.description: Using a subquery as a temporary table. Alias is mandatory.
12_Subqueries_and_CTEs.example: SELECT AVG(total) FROM (SELECT user_id, SUM(amount) as total FROM orders GROUP BY user_id) AS user_totals;

12_Subqueries_and_CTEs.syntax: SELECT ... WHERE EXISTS (SELECT ... WHERE t1.col = t2.col)
12_Subqueries_and_CTEs.description: A subquery that references columns from the outer query.
12_Subqueries_and_CTEs.example: SELECT name FROM employees e WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM managers m WHERE m.id = e.manager_id);

12_Subqueries_and_CTEs.syntax: WITH alias AS (SELECT ...) SELECT ... FROM alias;
12_Subqueries_and_CTEs.description: Defines a temporary result set that you can reference within a SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE statement.
12_Subqueries_and_CTEs.example: WITH Sales_CTE AS (SELECT region, SUM(amount) as total FROM sales GROUP BY region) SELECT * FROM Sales_CTE WHERE total > 1000;

13_DML_Insert_Update_Delete.syntax: INSERT INTO table (col1, col2) VALUES (val1, val2);
INSERT INTO table (col1, col2) VALUES (v1, v2), (v3, v4), ...;
INSERT INTO table1 (col1) SELECT col2 FROM table2;
13_DML_Insert_Update_Delete.description: Copies data from one table to another.
13_DML_Insert_Update_Delete.example: INSERT INTO archived_users (id, name) SELECT id, name FROM users WHERE status = 'deleted';

13_DML_Insert_Update_Delete.description: Skips rows that cause duplicate key errors.

13_DML_Insert_Update_Delete.syntax: INSERT INTO table (...) VALUES (...) ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE col=expr;
13_DML_Insert_Update_Delete.description: Upsert: Updates the row if the primary/unique key already exists.
13_DML_Insert_Update_Delete.example: INSERT INTO page_views (page_id, views) VALUES (101, 1) ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE views = views + 1;

13_DML_Insert_Update_Delete.syntax: UPDATE table SET col = val WHERE condition;
13_DML_Insert_Update_Delete.example: UPDATE users SET active = 1 WHERE verified = 1;

13_DML_Insert_Update_Delete.syntax: UPDATE table SET col1 = val1, col2 = val2 WHERE condition;
13_DML_Insert_Update_Delete.example: UPDATE products SET price = 10, stock = 50 WHERE id = 5;

13_DML_Insert_Update_Delete.syntax: UPDATE table SET col = val WHERE condition LIMIT n;
13_DML_Insert_Update_Delete.description: Updates only the first n rows matching the condition.
13_DML_Insert_Update_Delete.example: UPDATE users SET status = 'batch_1' WHERE status = 'pending' LIMIT 100;

13_DML_Insert_Update_Delete.description: Removes all rows (slower than TRUNCATE, transaction-safe).

13_DML_Insert_Update_Delete.syntax: DELETE FROM table WHERE condition LIMIT n;
13_DML_Insert_Update_Delete.example: DELETE FROM queue WHERE status = 'processed' LIMIT 1000;

13_DML_Insert_Update_Delete.syntax: DELETE t1 FROM t1 JOIN t2 ON t1.ref = t2.id WHERE t2.condition;
13_DML_Insert_Update_Delete.description: Deletes rows from t1 based on conditions in t2.
13_DML_Insert_Update_Delete.example: DELETE o FROM orders o JOIN users u ON o.user_id = u.id WHERE u.is_banned = 1;

14_String_Functions_Detailed.description: Concatenates strings. Returns NULL if any argument is NULL.
Returns the length of the string in bytes.
Returns the length of the string in characters (multibyte safe).
Replaces all occurrences of a substring.
Returns the position of the first occurrence of substring.
Formats a number X to a format like '#,###,###.##' rounded to D decimals.

15_Numeric_Math_Functions.description: Smallest integer not less than x.
Largest integer not greater than x.
Rounds x to d decimal places.
Truncates x to d decimal places (no rounding).
Returns x raised to the power of y.
Returns a random float between 0 and 1.

16_Date_Time_Functions_Detailed.description: Adds a time value to a date.
Subtracts a time value from a date.
Returns number of days between date1 and date2.
Returns the day of the month (1-31).
Returns the name of the weekday.
Returns the last day of the month for the given date.
Converts a string to a date.

17_JSON_Functions_Detailed.description: Extracts data from a JSON document.
Creates a JSON object from key-value pairs.
Returns 1 if candidate is found in target.
Returns an array of keys from the top-level value.
Inserts or updates data in a JSON document.
Inserts data without replacing existing values.

18_Encryption_and_Information.description: Returns current user and host provided authentication.

19_Procedural_SQL_Advanced.description: Declares a local variable inside a stored program.
Assigns a value to a variable.

19_Procedural_SQL_Advanced.syntax: SELECT col INTO var_name FROM table ...;
19_Procedural_SQL_Advanced.description: Stores query result into a variable.
19_Procedural_SQL_Advanced.example: SELECT COUNT(*) INTO total FROM users;

19_Procedural_SQL_Advanced.syntax: IF search_condition THEN statement_list [ELSEIF ... THEN ...] [ELSE ...] END IF;
19_Procedural_SQL_Advanced.example: IF total > 100 THEN SET status = 'high'; ELSE SET status = 'low'; END IF;

19_Procedural_SQL_Advanced.syntax: CASE case_value WHEN when_value THEN statements ... ELSE statements END CASE;
19_Procedural_SQL_Advanced.example: CASE type WHEN 'A' THEN SET val=1; WHEN 'B' THEN SET val=2; END CASE;

19_Procedural_SQL_Advanced.syntax: WHILE search_condition DO statement_list END WHILE;
19_Procedural_SQL_Advanced.example: WHILE i < 10 DO SET i = i + 1; END WHILE;

19_Procedural_SQL_Advanced.syntax: REPEAT statement_list UNTIL search_condition END REPEAT;
19_Procedural_SQL_Advanced.example: REPEAT SET i = i + 1; UNTIL i >= 10 END REPEAT;

19_Procedural_SQL_Advanced.description: Exits or Restarts a labeled loop.
19_Procedural_SQL_Advanced.example: my_loop: LOOP ... IF done THEN LEAVE my_loop; END IF; END LOOP;
DECLARE cur CURSOR FOR SELECT id FROM users;

19_Procedural_SQL_Advanced.syntax: DECLARE handler_type HANDLER FOR condition_value statement;
19_Procedural_SQL_Advanced.example: DECLARE CONTINUE HANDLER FOR NOT FOUND SET done = TRUE;
